metadata.description: 信号量: P(V)/Wait/Signal. Managing multiple resources. Classic problem: Producer-Consumer. Here is a brief explanation and a corresponding Python code example for the producer-consumer problem: In this context, P(V) and V(V) are semaphore operations used to manage access to shared resources. The `P` operation (also known as Wait or Decrement) decreases the value of the semaphore by one; if the new value is less than zero, it blocks the process until the semaphore's value becomes non-negative. The `V` operation (also known as Signal or Increment) increases the semaphore's value by one and wakes up a waiting process if there are any. Python code example: ```python import threading class ProducerConsumer: def __init__(self): self.buffer = [] self.lock = threading.Lock() self.not_empty = threading.Semaphore(0) self.not_full = threading.Semaphore(10) # Maximum buffer size def produce(self, item): self.not_full.acquire() # P(not_full) self.lock.acquire() self.buffer.append(item) self.lock.release() self.not_empty.release() # V(not_empty) def consume(self): self.not_empty.acquire() # P(not_empty) self.lock.acquire() item = self.buffer.pop(0) self.lock.release() self.not_full.release() # V(not_full) return item # Example usage producer_consumer = ProducerConsumer() def producer(): for i in range(10): producer_consumer.produce(i) def consumer(): for _ in range(10): print(producer_consumer.consume()) # Running the example (in a real scenario, you would run these in separate threads) consumer() # This will block until items are produced ``` In this Python code: - `not_empty` and `not_full` semaphores manage the buffer's state. - The producer function uses `produce` to add an item to the buffer. - The consumer function uses `consume` to remove an item from the buffer.

Here is a brief explanation and a corresponding Python code example for the producer-consumer problem: In this context, P(V) and V(V) are semaphore operations used to manage access to shared resources. The `P` operation (also known as Wait or Decrement) decreases the value of the semaphore by one; if the new value is less than zero, it blocks the process until the semaphore's value becomes non-negative. The `V` operation (also known as Signal or Increment) increases the semaphore's value by one and wakes up a waiting process if there are any. Python code example: ```python import threading class ProducerConsumer: def __init__(self): self.buffer = [] self.lock = threading.Lock() self.not_empty = threading.Semaphore(0) self.not_full = threading.Semaphore(10) # Maximum buffer size def produce(self, item): self.not_full.acquire() # P(not_full) self.lock.acquire() self.buffer.append(item) self.lock.release() self.not_empty.release() # V(not_empty) def consume(self): self.not_empty.acquire() # P(not_empty) self.lock.acquire() item = self.buffer.pop(0) self.lock.release() self.not_full.release() # V(not_full) return item # Example usage producer_consumer = ProducerConsumer() def producer(): for i in range(10): producer_consumer.produce(i) def consumer(): for _ in range(10): print(producer_consumer.consume()) # Running the example (in a real scenario, you would run these in separate threads) consumer() # This will block until items are produced ``` In this Python code: - `not_empty` and `not_full` semaphores manage the buffer's state. - The producer function uses `produce` to add an item to the buffer. - The consumer function uses `consume` to remove an item from the buffer.
